title: Port Horizon
event: A labor disruption closes the main container terminal for several days.
why: Ports connect overseas suppliers to domestic transportation networks. A closure can delay goods before they reach rail, trucks, warehouses, or stores.
how: The disruption creates vessel backlogs, equipment shortages, missed delivery windows, and higher rerouting costs.
when: The risk grows during peak shipping seasons, labor disputes, major storms, cyber incidents, or equipment failures.
where: The first effects appear at the port, but delays can spread to inland rail terminals, distribution centers, factories, and retailers.
question: Which response best reduces the immediate cascading risk?
options: Wait for the port to reopen and keep all shipments unchanged
Use a secondary port and reserve rail or truck capacity before rerouting
Cancel every inbound order regardless of priority
takeaway: Redundancy works only when alternate routes and transportation capacity are arranged before the main node fails.

title: Riverbend Rail Junction
event: Floodwater closes a rail corridor that carries food, fuel, and industrial materials.
why: Rail corridors move high volumes efficiently, so one damaged segment can affect multiple industries at the same time.
how: A washed-out section blocks trains, fills nearby yards, and pushes freight toward roads that may not have enough capacity.
when: The risk rises during prolonged rain, rapid snowmelt, tropical systems, or when drainage and bridge systems are already stressed.
where: Effects begin along the flooded corridor and spread to ports, factories, farms, warehouses, fuel terminals, and communities that depend on the route.
question: What is the strongest first planning decision?
options: Prioritize critical cargo and activate pre-identified road or alternate rail routes
Move all cargo to trucks without checking road capacity
Keep dispatching trains toward the closed segment
takeaway: Prioritization matters because alternate routes usually have less capacity than the primary corridor.

title: Skybridge Airport
event: Wildfire smoke reduces visibility and creates unsafe working conditions near a cargo airport.
why: Air cargo is often used for urgent, high-value, medical, or time-sensitive goods.
how: Smoke can delay flights, reduce ramp activity, affect worker health, close roads, and slow ground transportation.
when: The risk is highest during active wildfire periods, shifting winds, temperature inversions, and regional evacuations.
where: The disruption affects the airport and nearby roads first, then spreads to hospitals, manufacturers, repair operations, and customers waiting for urgent items.
question: Which option protects continuity without ignoring worker safety?
options: Keep full operations running regardless of air quality
Pause all regional deliveries for the rest of the month
Shift urgent cargo to a safer airport and use air-quality thresholds for local work
takeaway: Continuity plans must protect workers while identifying alternate nodes for truly urgent cargo.

title: Northstar Logistics Hub
event: A cyberattack disrupts shipment scheduling, gate appointments, and inventory visibility.
why: Physical cargo movement depends on digital systems for scheduling, documentation, routing, and tracking.
how: When data is unavailable or untrusted, trucks may queue, containers may be misplaced, and partners may stop accepting automated instructions.
when: The risk can appear after phishing, weak access controls, vulnerable software, compromised vendors, or poor network separation.
where: The initial system outage may be local, but connected carriers, ports, warehouses, suppliers, and customers can all lose visibility.
question: Which response best limits operational and cyber risk?
options: Use a tested manual fallback, isolate affected systems, and verify data before restoration
Reconnect every system immediately without checking the cause
Post administrator credentials in the team chat so work can continue
takeaway: Resilience includes tested manual procedures, network isolation, verified restoration, and controlled access.

title: Cedar Distribution Center
event: A regional fuel shortage reduces available trucking capacity.
why: Warehouses depend on reliable inbound and outbound transportation. Inventory cannot help communities if it cannot move.
how: Fuel shortages increase costs, reduce trip frequency, and force carriers to prioritize routes and customers.
when: The risk rises after refinery outages, pipeline problems, severe weather, conflict, cyber incidents, or sudden demand spikes.
where: The shortage affects carriers and fuel terminals first, then spreads to warehouses, stores, hospitals, farms, and emergency response operations.
question: Which action best protects essential deliveries?
options: Prioritize critical loads, combine trips, and coordinate fuel access with partners
Send every truck on its normal route even when loads are nearly empty
Stop sharing status information with partners
takeaway: Resource sharing and priority rules help limited transportation capacity support the most important needs first.
